content:
# Matthew Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_010_page_001.jpg, MAT_010_page_002.jpg

### Jesus Sends Out the Twelve

1. Jesus called his twelve disciples together and gave them authority to drive out evil spirits and to heal every kind of disease and sickness.

2. These are the names of the twelve apostles: first, Simon (called Peter) and his brother Andrew; James son of Zebedee and his brother John;

3. Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus;

4. Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot — the one who would betray him.

5. Jesus sent these twelve out with these instructions: "Don't go to Gentile regions, and don't enter any Samaritan town.

6. Instead, go to the lost sheep of the people of Israel.

7. As you go, announce this: 'The kingdom of heaven is near!'

8. Heal the sick, raise the dead, cure those with leprosy, drive out demons. You received all of this as a gift — now give it as a gift.

9. Don't take along any gold, silver, or copper coins.

10. Don't carry a travel bag, an extra coat, sandals, or a walking stick. The worker deserves to be fed.

11. When you enter a town or village, look for someone trustworthy and stay with them until you move on.

12. When you enter the home, give it your greeting of peace.

13. If the home is receptive, let your peace rest on it; if not, take your peace back with you.

14. If any house or town refuses to welcome you or hear your message, leave that place and shake the dust off your feet.

15. I tell you the truth: Sodom and Gomorrah will be better off on judgment day than that town.

### Warnings About Persecution

16. Listen — I'm sending you out like sheep among wolves. Be as shrewd as snakes and as innocent as doves.

17. Watch out for people. They will hand you over to local councils and have you flogged in their synagogues.

18. You will be hauled before governors and kings because of me — and that will be your opportunity to testify before them and the nations.

19. When they arrest you, don't worry about what to say or how to say it. The right words will be given to you at the right time.

20. It won't be you speaking — it will be the Spirit of your Father speaking through you.

21. Brothers will betray brothers to death, and fathers their children. Children will turn against their parents and have them killed.

22. Everyone will hate you because of me. But the one who stands firm to the end will be saved.

23. When you're persecuted in one town, escape to the next. I tell you the truth: you won't have made it through all the towns of Israel before the Son of Man comes.

24. A student is not above the teacher, and a servant is not above the master.

25. It's enough for students to become like their teacher, and servants like their master. If they called the head of the house 'Beelzebul,' imagine what they'll call his family!

### Fear God, Not People

26. So don't be afraid of them. Everything that's concealed will be brought to light, and every secret will be made known.

27. What I whisper to you in the dark, shout from the rooftops. What's spoken in your ear, announce publicly.

28. Don't fear those who can kill your body but can't touch your soul. Instead, fear the one who can destroy both body and soul in hell.

29. Aren't two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them falls to the ground outside your Father's care.

30. And every hair on your head has been counted.

31. So don't be afraid — you're worth more than a whole flock of sparrows.

32. Everyone who stands up for me before others, I will stand up for before my Father in heaven.

33. But everyone who disowns me before others, I will disown before my Father in heaven.

### The Cost of Following Jesus

34. Don't imagine I came to bring peace to the world. I didn't come to bring peace — I came to bring a sword.

35. I came to turn a man against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.

36. Your worst enemies will be the people in your own home.

37. If you love your father or mother more than me, you're not worthy of me. If you love your son or daughter more than me, you're not worthy of me.

38. If you refuse to pick up your cross and follow me, you're not worthy of me.

39. If you cling to your life, you'll lose it. But if you let go of your life for my sake, you'll find it.

### Rewards for Hospitality

40. Anyone who welcomes you welcomes me, and anyone who welcomes me welcomes the one who sent me.

41. Whoever welcomes a prophet because they are a prophet will receive a prophet's reward. Whoever welcomes a righteous person because they are righteous will receive a righteous person's reward.

42. And if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these humble ones because they follow me — I tell you the truth, they will not lose their reward."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 5-6 — "Don't go to Gentile regions":** This restriction is temporary — Matthew's Gospel ends with Jesus sending his followers to "all nations" (28:19). At this stage, Jesus limits the mission to Israel. The movement from Israel-only to all-nations is a key arc of Matthew's narrative.

**Verse 16 — "shrewd as snakes":** The combination is deliberately paradoxical. Snakes in Jewish tradition represent cunning (Genesis 3), while doves represent purity and innocence. Jesus wants his followers to be simultaneously streetwise and morally pure — neither naïve nor manipulative.

**Verse 28 — "destroy both body and soul in hell":** One of Jesus' starkest warnings. The word for "destroy" (ἀπολέσαι) implies ruin and loss, not annihilation. The fear of God is presented as the antidote to the fear of human persecution.

**Verse 34 — "a sword":** Jesus overturns expectations. The Messiah was expected to bring peace to Israel. Instead, Jesus says his message will divide — even families. The "sword" is not violence but the sharp division that loyalty to Jesus inevitably creates.

**Verse 38 — "pick up your cross":** The first time Jesus mentions the cross in Matthew. Before Calvary, a cross meant one thing: Roman execution. To "pick up your cross" means to accept public shame and death as the cost of following him. This would have been shocking to first-century hearers.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
